questions.question: What fundamental assumption breaks when multiple learning agents share an environment?
options: Rewards can no longer be scalar

Stationarity: each agent is part of the others' environment, so as policies change the transition dynamics shift — the Markov property fails because next state depends on other agents' moving-target choices, voiding tabular convergence proofs
Gradients can't be computed
Actions become continuous
questions.explanation: Naive deep RL chases itself in loops and never converges. MARL is the set of tricks — CTDE, counterfactual baselines, self-play, leagues — that restore learnability.

questions.question: What is a Markov Game, and how do reward structures classify it?
options: An MDP played on a game board

The MDP generalization with joint actions a = (a_1...a_n), transitions P(s'|s,a), and PER-AGENT rewards — identical rewards make it fully cooperative, zero-sum makes it adversarial, anything else is general-sum
A card game used for benchmarks
Any game with random transitions
questions.explanation: The reward structure dictates the algorithm family: cooperative settings suit centralized critics and value decomposition; adversarial ones suit self-play and leagues.

questions.question: What is centralized training / decentralized execution (CTDE)?
options: One agent controls all the others

Each agent's POLICY conditions only on its local observation (deployable decentralized), but during training a centralized critic sees the global state and joint action — MADDPG, COMA's counterfactual baseline, MAPPO, and QMIX's monotonic value decomposition are all instances
Training on a central server farm
Sharing one policy across all agents
questions.explanation: The dominant modern paradigm: privileged information stabilizes training, then gets discarded at deployment. MAPPO with a shared critic dominates cooperative MARL in 2026.

questions.question: When does league play improve on plain self-play?
options: When compute is limited

When the game has rock-paper-scissors strategy cycles: a population of past and current policies plus exploiters (beat the current best) and main-exploiters (beat the exploiters) prevents cycling — AlphaStar's recipe for StarCraft II
Only in two-player perfect-information games
League play is always strictly worse
questions.explanation: Self-play (AlphaZero, OpenAI Five) works best in zero-sum symmetric settings; once strategies can cycle, training against a diverse league is what forces robustness.

questions.question: Which core challenges define MARL beyond non-stationarity?
options: Only GPU memory limits

Credit assignment under shared rewards (who caused it?), exploration coordination (complementary not redundant), joint-action-space blowup exponential in n, and partial observability of the global state
Choosing the right random seed
Reward normalization only
questions.explanation: COMA's counterfactual baseline ("what if I'd acted differently?") targets credit assignment directly; QMIX's decomposition targets scalability. Each technique maps to one named challenge.
